original_hypothesis.title: Bridge staticcall Prevents SignalService Proof Caching, Causing Valid Proofs to Revert
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Bridge._proveSignalReceived uses staticcall to invoke SignalService.proveSignalReceived. However, proveSignalReceived may call _syncChainData (which performs SSTORE) when CacheOption is non-zero. SSTORE in a staticcall context causes the entire staticcall to revert, making the proof appear invalid. This means any proof submitted with cache options through Bridge will be silently rejected.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The code has a fundamental mismatch: SignalService.proveSignalReceived is designed to optionally cache chain data (state modifications), but Bridge calls it via staticcall (which prohibits state modifications). Proofs with CacheOption != CACHE_NOTHING will always fail when verified through Bridge.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _signalService.staticcall(data) where data encodes proveSignalReceived which may SSTORE via _cacheChainData

preconditions: Relayer constructs HopProof with CacheOption != CACHE_NOTHING
The proof path includes a hop with cacheStateRoot or cacheSignalRoot conditions met

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: staticcall + SSTORE = revert; all non-CACHE_NOTHING proofs fail through Bridge
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayers unaware of this limitation submit proofs with caching, which fail silently. Messages appear unprocessable.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS vector: attacker could confuse relayers or block specific message processing if relayers use caching by default

economic_questions: Do existing relayer implementations default to CACHE_NOTHING?
Can an attacker construct a proof that FORCES caching via specific hop conditions?
Is the caching feature intended to be used only via direct SignalService calls?
validation_requirements: Submit processMessage with a proof having CACHE_BOTH and verify it reverts with B_NOT_RECEIVED
Verify same proof succeeds with CACHE_NOTHING
Check if any non-last-hop condition forces _syncChainData even with CACHE_NOTHING

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; DoS impact on message processing
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Persistent until relayers learn to use CACHE_NOTHING

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users whose messages are processed with caching proofs
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Message stuck until re-proven with CACHE_NOTHING
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All messages attempted with non-zero CacheOption

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test CacheOption values through Bridge's staticcall path
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm SSTORE in staticcall causes revert

validation_result.mechanism: staticcall incompatible with SSTORE in caching path
validation_result.impact_summary: Bridge._proveSignalReceived uses staticcall to invoke SignalService.proveSignalReceived. When HopProof.cacheOption != CACHE_NOTHING and caching conditions are met, SSTORE/LOG opcodes execute inside the staticcall, causing silent revert. Valid proofs with caching appear as invalid.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The Bridge contract's _proveSignalReceived function (Bridge.sol:342-353) uses staticcall to invoke SignalService.proveSignalReceived. The SignalService function iterates over HopProofs and calls _cacheChainData, which conditionally calls _syncChainData. _syncChainData performs SSTORE operations (writing signal slots and topBlockId) and emits LOG events. Both SSTORE and LOG are prohibited within a staticcall context per EVM specification, causing the entire staticcall to revert. The Bridge interprets the revert as proof failure (success_ = false), leading to B_NOT_RECEIVED or B_NOT_FAILED errors.

The CacheOption is part of the HopProof struct encoded in the _proof bytes parameter, controlled by whoever submits the proof (typically a relayer). For single-hop proofs with accountProof.length > 0 (full proof), setting cacheOption to CACHE_SIGNAL_ROOT or CACHE_BOTH triggers caching on the last hop via: cacheSignalRoot && (_isFullProof || !_isLastHop). For multi-hop proofs, non-last hops with any non-CACHE_NOTHING option trigger caching more readily.

This means the caching feature of SignalService.proveSignalReceived is completely unusable when called through the Bridge, despite being a designed feature of the protocol. Any relayer that constructs proofs with caching enabled will have their proofs silently rejected.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Bridge.sol:342-353 clearly shows staticcall. SignalService.sol _cacheChainData (lines around 255-275) shows conditional SSTORE paths via _syncChainData. _syncChainData (lines around 220-230) performs sstore and emits events. The code path from staticcall to SSTORE is direct and unconditional when CacheOption != CACHE_NOTHING and hop conditions are met.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Uses staticcall to invoke proveSignalReceived, which may perform SSTORE operations when CacheOption != CACHE_NOTHING. SSTORE within staticcall causes the call to revert, making valid proofs with caching appear invalid.
Conditionally calls _syncChainData which performs SSTORE. When called within a staticcall context (from Bridge), any SSTORE causes the entire call to revert.
Performs SSTORE via _sendSignal (assembly sstore), writes to topBlockId mapping (SSTORE), and emits events (LOG). All three are prohibited in staticcall context.

calling_sequence: 5. If CacheOption != CACHE_NOTHING and conditions met, _syncChainData is called (SignalService.sol:~265-270)
7. SSTORE in staticcall context causes EVM revert
9. Bridge sees success_ = false, reverts with B_NOT_RECEIVED
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Bridge.processMessage -> Bridge._proveSignalReceived -> staticcall -> SignalService.proveSignalReceived -> SignalService._cacheChainData -> SignalService._syncChainData -> _sendSignal -> assembly { sstore(slot_, _value) } -> EVM revert due to staticcall context. The revert propagates back as success_=false, causing Bridge to revert with B_NOT_RECEIVED. This happens when: (a) for single-hop with full proof (accountProof.length>0) and cacheOption includes CACHE_SIGNAL_ROOT or CACHE_BOTH, or (b) for any non-last hop with non-CACHE_NOTHING cacheOption.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this issue. The defect is inherent in the code architecture - Bridge uses staticcall while SignalService has state-modifying code paths.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A relayer or user constructs a valid Merkle proof for a bridge message and includes CacheOption.CACHE_SIGNAL_ROOT (or CACHE_BOTH) in the HopProof for performance optimization. When they call Bridge.processMessage, the _proveSignalReceived function invokes SignalService via staticcall. The SignalService attempts to cache the signal root by calling _syncChainData, which executes SSTORE. The EVM immediately reverts the staticcall. The Bridge receives false from the staticcall and reverts with B_NOT_RECEIVED. The message remains unprocessed in NEW status. The same proof would succeed if submitted with CACHE_NOTHING. This is not an attack per se but a design defect that silently breaks a protocol feature and can cause confusion for relayers.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The defect makes the caching feature of SignalService completely unusable through Bridge interactions (processMessage, recallMessage, proveMessageFailed, proveMessageReceived). Relayers who attempt to use caching will have their proofs silently rejected. Messages can still be processed using CACHE_NOTHING, so this is not a complete DoS. However: (1) the caching optimization intended to reduce future proof costs cannot be leveraged through Bridge, (2) relayers unaware of this limitation waste gas on failed transactions, (3) there's a mismatch between the SignalService API (which offers caching) and the Bridge's usage of it.
recommended_mitigations: Change Bridge._proveSignalReceived to use a regular call instead of staticcall, and check the return value appropriately. This would allow caching to work as designed.
Alternatively, add a separate view-only version of proveSignalReceived in SignalService that skips caching (always uses CACHE_NOTHING internally), and have Bridge call that version.

If staticcall is intentionally used to prevent state changes during proof verification (defensive design), document this limitation and ensure the HopProof.cacheOption is stripped/ignored when called from Bridge, or add validation in SignalService that detects staticcall context and skips caching.
Add prominent documentation warning relayers to always use CACHE_NOTHING when submitting proofs through Bridge.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Bridge and SignalService on a test chain. 2. Sync chain data for a remote chain to establish a valid state root. 3. Construct a valid HopProof with CacheOption.CACHE_SIGNAL_ROOT and accountProof populated. 4. Call Bridge.processMessage with this proof - expect B_NOT_RECEIVED revert. 5. Change the same proof's CacheOption to CACHE_NOTHING. 6. Call Bridge.processMessage again - expect success. 7. This demonstrates the staticcall/SSTORE incompatibility.
uncertainties: Whether any production relayer implementations currently use non-CACHE_NOTHING options through Bridge (practical impact depends on real relayer behavior)
Whether the staticcall usage was an intentional security decision to prevent state modifications during proof verification, making this a design tradeoff rather than a bug
additional_testing_needed: Verify the exact CacheOption values that trigger SSTORE for single-hop vs multi-hop proofs
Test whether LOG (emit) alone within staticcall also causes revert (it does per EVM spec)
Check if any existing tests cover non-CACHE_NOTHING proofs through Bridge
Audit relayer implementations to determine if any use caching through Bridge in production
